title: Portfolio Website
text:
Welcome to my portfolio website! This site showcases my work as a Data Scientist and AI Engineer, highlighting my skills, projects, and professional journey.


This portfolio is designed to provide an overview of my expertise in data science and artificial intelligence. It includes detailed descriptions of the projects I have worked on, the tools and methodologies I have used, and the impact of my work.



Each project is presented with the following structure:

Problem Description: A brief overview of the problem the project aimed to solve.
Approach: The methodology or strategy used to address the problem.
Tools and Actions: The technologies, tools, and actions employed during the project.
Results: The outcomes of the project, including key metrics or achievements.
Impact: The broader implications or benefits of the project.




You can find the source code and additional details for this portfolio website in the GitHub repository.

Feel free to explore the site and reach out if you have any questions or opportunities to collaborate!

title: Professional Experience
text:
Role: Data Scientist Scope: ~250,000 customers Measurable impact: NPS 66 → 71 (+5 points) in 12 months Context Quilmes used NPS as its key customer satisfaction metric, but due to volume constraints it surveyed only around 10% of its customer base each month. This left the majority of customers without visibility and made it difficult to prioritize preventive actions. Challenge
Measure “customer health” without surveys for most of the base. Turn a statistical prediction into an operational, business-friendly indicator. Integrate scattered signals (sales, logistics, customer support, app usage) into a single score.
Solution (what I built) I designed a 0–100 Health Score for the entire portfolio, based on the probability of being a Promoter (NPS 9–10):
Trained a binary classification model (Logistic Regression) to estimate promoter vs. non-promoter probability. Used model coefficients to build an interpretable, weighted score (0–100). Defined actionable ranges (low / medium / high health) that triggered different strategies.
Implementation (how I did it) Cross-functional data sources:
Sales: recency/frequency, volume variation, product mix, average ticket Logistics: fulfillment, incidents, delays, delivery quality Customer support: complaints, resolution time, recurrence App & rewards: engagement, benefit adoption, usage habits
Interpretable model: Logistic Regression enabled clear explanations of what drives the score. Business translation: I converted the prediction into a simple 0–100 score and defined thresholds together with end users to ensure actionability. Results
NPS increased from 66 to 71 (+5) during May 2022 – May 2023. The organization moved from working with a partial signal (10% surveyed) to full coverage with a per-customer score. Improved prioritization: low-health customers received focused, timely corrective strategies.
Why this was key
Choosing an explainable model accelerated adoption: the business trusts what it understands. Combining operational signals (logistics + support) had a real impact on satisfaction, beyond sales alone.
What to showcase in a portfolio (ideal)
A simple diagram: Sources → Features → Model → Health Score → Actions An example of “Top drivers” (without sensitive data) A before/after NPS chart plus the health score distribution

title: About
text:
Soy Leonardo Vela, Data Scientist. Me enfoco en proyectos con impacto de negocio, desde el baseline hasta despliegue y MLOps.
Stack: Python, SQL, Azure/Databricks, MLflow, etc.

GitHub: https://github.com/andresleonardovela
LinkedIn: https://linkedin.com/in/leonardovela
